sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: Researchers perfused isolated mammalian nephrons to test Drug X, a compound that markedly increased urinary glucose and urine flow while lowering proximal sodium reabsorption. Adding aldosterone after Drug X did not restore glucose reabsorption. ATP levels in tubular cells were normal, but ouabain, an inhibitor of the Na+/K+ ATPase, abolished the remaining sodium-linked glucose uptake in control nephrons.
questions.stem: Which transporter is Drug X most likely inhibiting?

choices.C: A loop of Henle Na+-K+-2Cl- transporter

explanations.A: Correct. The figure shows increased urinary glucose with reduced sodium reabsorption, fitting inhibition of proximal tubule SGLT-mediated secondary active transport.
explanations.B: Aquaporin inhibition would change water permeability but would not directly cause marked glucosuria.
explanations.C: Blocking NKCC in the thick ascending limb would affect medullary gradients, not glucose reabsorption.
explanations.D: Aldosterone acts later in the nephron and would not explain glucosuria.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Transport and urine changes after Drug X.

questions.stem: Why did Drug X increase urine flow?

choices.B: Unreabsorbed glucose osmotically retained water in the tubular lumen
choices.A: Aldosterone forced water into the filtrate
choices.C: Drug X directly hydrolyzed ATP to make water
choices.D: Glucose diffused into blood and carried water with it

explanations.B: Correct. Solute remaining in the filtrate increases osmotic pressure and reduces water reabsorption, producing osmotic diuresis.
explanations.A: Aldosterone increases sodium reabsorption and tends to favor water retention indirectly.
explanations.C: Hydrolysis of ATP does not generate the urine flow effect described.
explanations.D: The figure shows glucose in urine, not increased glucose diffusion into blood.

questions.stem: Aldosterone failed to reverse Drug X because aldosterone primarily acts on which nephron region?

choices.C: Late distal tubule and collecting duct
choices.D: Descending limb of the loop of Henle

explanations.C: Correct. Aldosterone increases ENaC and Na+/K+ ATPase activity mainly in principal cells of the late distal tubule and collecting duct.
explanations.A: The glomerulus filters plasma; it is not the principal site of aldosterone-driven sodium reabsorption.
explanations.B: Glucose reabsorption is largely proximal, but aldosterone is not primarily a brush-border proximal tubule hormone.
explanations.D: The descending limb is water-permeable and not the major aldosterone target.

questions.stem: Ouabain abolishing remaining glucose uptake supports which conclusion?

choices.D: Na+-glucose transport depends indirectly on the Na+ gradient maintained by Na+/K+ ATPase
choices.A: Glucose uptake is simple diffusion down a glucose gradient
choices.C: Glucose enters tubular cells through aquaporins

explanations.D: Correct. SGLT uses the inward Na+ gradient created by basolateral Na+/K+ ATPase, so inhibiting the pump disrupts secondary active glucose uptake.
explanations.A: The ouabain sensitivity indicates dependence on an ion gradient rather than simple diffusion.
explanations.B: Ouabain inhibits Na+/K+ ATPase; it is not an aldosterone agonist.

questions.stem: Which change would most directly increase glucose reabsorption in untreated proximal tubules?

choices.A: Increasing the basolateral Na+/K+ ATPase gradient-driving capacity
choices.D: Lowering luminal glucose concentration to zero

explanations.A: Correct. A stronger Na+ gradient would increase the driving force for Na+-coupled glucose uptake, provided transporters are available.
explanations.B: Reduced ATP would weaken Na+/K+ ATPase activity.
explanations.C: Blocking apical Na+ entry would impair SGLT.
explanations.D: With no luminal glucose, there is no substrate to reabsorb.

questions.passage: A patient with severe diarrhea developed fatigue and rapid breathing. Blood tests showed decreased plasma bicarbonate, decreased pH, and a lower-than-normal arterial PCO2 after several hours. Urine collected later in the day contained increased NH4+ and titratable acid. Clinicians concluded that the primary disturbance was metabolic and that respiratory and renal compensation had begun.
questions.stem: Which compensatory response explains the decreased PCO2?

explanations.B: Correct. Rapid breathing lowers arterial CO2, reducing carbonic acid and partially raising pH in metabolic acidosis.
explanations.A: Hypoventilation would increase PCO2 and worsen acidemia.
explanations.C: Increased, not reduced, ammonium excretion helps renal compensation.
explanations.D: Losing more bicarbonate would worsen the metabolic acidosis.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Blood gas values during a metabolic acidosis and partial compensation.

questions.stem: What is the primary acid-base disturbance?

choices.C: Metabolic acidosis caused by bicarbonate loss
choices.A: Respiratory acidosis caused by CO2 retention
choices.B: Metabolic alkalosis caused by bicarbonate excess
choices.D: Respiratory alkalosis caused by primary hyperventilation

explanations.C: Correct. Diarrhea can remove bicarbonate-rich fluid, and the primary low HCO3- with low pH identifies metabolic acidosis.
explanations.B: The pH and bicarbonate are low, not elevated.
explanations.D: Hyperventilation is compensatory because bicarbonate loss is the primary disturbance.

questions.stem: The later urinary findings most directly indicate which renal response?

choices.D: Increased H+ secretion coupled to generation of new bicarbonate
choices.B: Decreased acid excretion to preserve plasma pH

explanations.D: Correct. In metabolic acidosis, kidneys increase ammonium and titratable acid excretion while regenerating bicarbonate.
explanations.A: Increased NH4+ and titratable acid are signs of active compensation.
explanations.B: Decreasing acid excretion would worsen acidosis.
explanations.C: Bicarbonate is not converted into glucose in this compensation process.

questions.stem: How would the initial low pH affect hemoglobin oxygen binding?

choices.A: It would shift the oxygen dissociation curve to the right
choices.B: It would prevent all oxygen binding
choices.C: It would shift the curve left by increasing hemoglobin affinity

explanations.A: Correct. Lower pH stabilizes deoxyhemoglobin and promotes oxygen unloading, producing a right shift by the Bohr effect.
explanations.B: Hemoglobin still binds oxygen; affinity changes rather than disappearing.
explanations.C: Low pH decreases affinity rather than increasing it.
explanations.D: The passage does not imply immediate elimination of 2,3-BPG.

questions.stem: Which response acts fastest after bicarbonate loss?

choices.B: Chemical buffering by extracellular and intracellular buffers
choices.A: Renal generation of new bicarbonate over days
choices.D: Bone remodeling to alter calcium release

explanations.B: Correct. Chemical buffers respond immediately, whereas respiratory compensation follows within minutes and renal compensation takes longer.
explanations.A: Renal compensation is important but slower.
explanations.C: Erythropoietin affects red blood cell production, not immediate acid-base buffering.
explanations.D: Bone buffering can contribute chronically but is not the fastest response.
